content:
{
  "title": "Fact-Chain Prompting: Structured Fact-Verification Chains to Reduce LLM Hallucination",
  "problem": "Large language models (LLMs) frequently generate hallucinated or unverifiable content when prompted for factual information, undermining trust and reliability in knowledge-intensive applications.",
  "motivation": "Improving LLM factuality is crucial for deploying these models in domains like science, medicine, and journalism. Existing prompting techniques often fail to systematically constrain generations to verifiable content. Developing prompting methods that explicitly bias models toward factual consistency is timely given recent advances in retrieval-augmented generation and growing demand for trustworthy AI.",
  "method": "We propose 'Fact-Chain Prompting,' where each user query is reformulated into a sequence of explicit intermediate factual sub-questions. The LLM is prompted to answer each sub-question separately, with instructions to cite an external trusted source (e.g., Wikipedia) for every answer. The final answer is synthesized only if all sub-answers are accompanied by verifiable citations. This method leverages chain-of-thought reasoning but anchors each step in external evidence, aiming to systematically suppress hallucination.",
  "experiment_plan": "We will evaluate Fact-Chain Prompting on subsets of the TruthfulQA and FEVER datasets. Baselines include standard prompting, chain-of-thought prompting, and retrieval-augmented prompting. Metrics will be factuality (using human and automated fact-checking), citation accuracy, and hallucination rate. The minimal experiment will compare output factuality for 50 questions under each prompting method."
}
